From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Consecration](/w/index.php?title=Consecration&redirect=no "Consecration"))

Dedicated or set apart for the service or worship of a deity

For other uses, see [Sacred (disambiguation)](/wiki/Sacred_(disambiguation) "Sacred (disambiguation)").

"Sanctity" redirects here. For other uses, see [Sanctity (disambiguation)](/wiki/Sanctity_(disambiguation) "Sanctity (disambiguation)").

**Sacred** describes something that is dedicated or set apart for the service or worship of a [deity](/wiki/Deity "Deity");[[1]](#cite_note-1) is considered worthy of spiritual [respect](/wiki/Respect "Respect") or devotion; or inspires [awe](/wiki/Awe "Awe") or [reverence](/wiki/Reverence_(emotion) "Reverence (emotion)") among believers. The property is often ascribed to objects (a "[sacred artifact](/wiki/Relic "Relic")" that is [venerated](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration") and [blessed](/wiki/Blessing "Blessing")), or places ("[sacred ground](/wiki/Sacred_site "Sacred site")").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *sacred* descends from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *[sacer](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sacer "wikt:sacer")*, referring to that which is '[consecrated](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration"), dedicated' or 'purified' to the gods or anything in their power, as well as to *[sacerdotes](/wiki/Glossary_of_ancient_Roman_religion#sacerdos "Glossary of ancient Roman religion")*.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Latin sacer is itself from [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European "Proto-Indo-European") *[\*seh₂k-](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/seh%E2%82%82k- "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/seh₂k-")* "sacred, ceremony, ritual".[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Holy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Holy")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

[Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has the text of the [1911 *Encyclopædia Britannica*](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition "Encyclopædia Britannica Eleventh Edition") article "[Holy](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/1911_Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica/Holy "wikisource:1911 Encyclopædia Britannica/Holy")".

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Wikiquote-logo.svg/40px-Wikiquote-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikiquote-logo.svg)

Wikiquote has quotations related to ***[Holiness](https://en.wikiquote.org/wiki/Holiness "q:Holiness")***.

"Holy" and "Holiness" redirect here. For other uses, see [Holy (disambiguation)](/wiki/Holy_(disambiguation) "Holy (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Divinity](/wiki/Divinity "Divinity").

"Hallow" redirects here. For other uses, see [Hallow (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hallow_(disambiguation) "Hallow (disambiguation)").

Although the English language terms *sacred* and *holy* are similar in meaning, and they are sometimes used interchangeably, they carry subtle differences.[[4]](#cite_note-difference-4) In the Christian tradition, *holiness* is generally used in relation to people and relationships, whereas *sacredness* is used in relation to objects, places, or happenings.[[5]](#cite_note-5) For example, a saint may be considered holy but not necessarily sacred. Nonetheless, some things can be both holy and sacred, such as the [Holy Bible](/wiki/Holy_Bible "Holy Bible").[[4]](#cite_note-difference-4)

Although *sacred* and *holy* denote something or someone set apart to the worship of God and therefore, worthy of respect and sometimes veneration, *holy* (the stronger word) implies an inherent or essential character.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Holiness originates in God and is communicated to things, places, times, and persons engaged in His Service. Thus, [Thomas Aquinas](/wiki/Thomas_Aquinas "Thomas Aquinas") defines *holiness* as that virtue by which a man's mind applies itself and all its acts to God; he ranks it among the infused moral [virtues](/wiki/Virtue "Virtue"), and identifies it with the virtue of religion. However, whereas religion is the virtue whereby one offers God due service in the things which pertain to the Divine service, holiness is the virtue by which one makes all one's acts subservient to God. Thus, holiness or sanctity is the outcome of [sanctification](/wiki/Sanctification "Sanctification"), that Divine act by which God freely justifies a person and by which He has claimed them for His own.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Etymology of 'holy'

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Etymology of 'holy'")]

The English word *holy* dates back to the [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic "Proto-Germanic") word *[hailagaz](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/hailagaz "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/hailagaz")* from around 500 BC, an adjective derived from *[hailaz](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/hailaz "wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Germanic/hailaz")* ('whole'), which was used to mean 'uninjured, sound, healthy, entire, complete'.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In non-specialist contexts, the term *holy* refers to someone or something that is associated with a [divine power](/wiki/Divine_grace "Divine grace"), such as [water](/wiki/Holy_water "Holy water") used for [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Transitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Transitions")]

"Consecration" redirects here. For other uses, see [Consecration (disambiguation)](/wiki/Consecration_(disambiguation) "Consecration (disambiguation)").

The concept of things being made or associated with the sacred is widespread among [religions](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), making people, places, and objects revered, set apart for special use or purpose, or transferred to the sacred sphere. Words for this include **hallow**, **sanctify**, and **consecrate**, which can be contrasted with [desecration](/wiki/Desecration "Desecration") and [deconsecration](/wiki/Deconsecration "Deconsecration"). These terms are used in various ways by different groups.

Sanctification and consecration come from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *sanctificare* (to set apart for special use or purpose, make holy or sacred)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) and *consecratus* (dedicated, devoted, and sacred).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Christianity")]

Further information: [Sanctification in Christianity](/wiki/Sanctification_in_Christianity "Sanctification in Christianity")

The verb form 'to hallow' is archaic in English, and does not appear other than in the quoted text in the [Lord's Prayer](/wiki/Lord%27s_Prayer "Lord's Prayer") in the New Testament.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) The noun form *hallow*, as used in *[Hallowtide](/wiki/Hallowtide "Hallowtide")*, is a synonym of the word [saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint").[[15]](#cite_note-WilsonFischer2005-15)[[16]](#cite_note-DiehlDonnelly2001-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Leslie1895-17)

In the various [branches](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") the details differ. [Sanctification in Christianity](/wiki/Sanctification_in_Christianity "Sanctification in Christianity") usually refers to a *person* becoming holy,[[18]](#cite_note-18) while [consecration in Christianity](/wiki/Consecration_in_Christianity "Consecration in Christianity") may include setting apart a person, [building](/wiki/Dedication_of_churches "Dedication of churches"), or object, for God. Among some Christian denominations there is a complementary service of "[deconsecration](/wiki/Deconsecration "Deconsecration")", to remove something consecrated of its sacred character in preparation for either demolition or sale for secular use.

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Judaism")]

See also: [Kiddush Hashem](/wiki/Kiddush_Hashem "Kiddush Hashem"), [The mitzvah of sanctifying the Kohen](/wiki/The_mitzvah_of_sanctifying_the_Kohen "The mitzvah of sanctifying the Kohen"), [Self-sacrifice in Jewish law](/wiki/Self-sacrifice_in_Jewish_law "Self-sacrifice in Jewish law"), and [Martyrdom in Judaism](/wiki/Martyrdom_in_Judaism "Martyrdom in Judaism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/High_Priest_Offering_Sacrifice_of_a_Goat.jpg/250px-High_Priest_Offering_Sacrifice_of_a_Goat.jpg)](/wiki/File:High_Priest_Offering_Sacrifice_of_a_Goat.jpg)

The High Priest offers the sacrifice of a goat performing *korban*

The Book of Exodus describes in detail the consecration of [Aaronite priests](/wiki/Kohen "Kohen") and cultic objects through washing, vesting, anointing, and sacrifice, concluding that both the priests and their garments become holy. Similarly, the altar is sanctified in such a way that anything that comes into contact with it also becomes [consecrated](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration"), rendering it unsuitable for ordinary use. A commentary on the Book of Leviticus reads, “Throughout Priestly thought, holiness is conceived of as an effervescence of the Presence of the Lord. It infuses everything with which it comes into contact ... transforming it into the designated ‘personal’ property of the deity."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In [rabbinic Judaism](/wiki/Rabbinic_Judaism "Rabbinic Judaism") sanctification means sanctifying God's name by [works of mercy](/wiki/Works_of_mercy "Works of mercy") and [martyrdom](/wiki/Martyrdom "Martyrdom"), while desecration of God's name means committing [sin](/wiki/Sin "Sin").[[20]](#cite_note-20) This is based on the Jewish concept of God, whose holiness is pure goodness and is transmissible by sanctifying people and things.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Islam")]

In [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), sanctification is termed as [tazkiah](/wiki/Tazkiah "Tazkiah"), other similarly used words to the term are *[Islah](/wiki/Islah "Islah")-i [qalb](/wiki/Qalb "Qalb")* (reform of the heart), *[Ihsan](/wiki/Ihsan "Ihsan")* (beautification), [taharat](/wiki/Taharat "Taharat") (purification), *[Ikhlas](/wiki/Ikhlas "Ikhlas")* (purity), *[qalb](/wiki/Qalb "Qalb")-is-[salim](/wiki/Salim_(name) "Salim (name)")* (pure/safe/undamaged heart). *[Tasawuf](/wiki/Tasawuf "Tasawuf")* (Sufism), basically an ideology rather than a term, is mostly misinterpreted as the idea of sanctification in Islam and it is used to pray about [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint"), especially among Sufis, in whom it is common to say "that God sanctifies his secret" ("qaddasa Llahou Sirruhu"), and that the Saint is alive or dead.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Buddhism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Buddhism")]

Images of the Buddha and [bodhisattvas](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva") are ceremonially consecrated in a broad range of Buddhist rituals that vary depending on the Buddhist traditions. [Buddhābhiseka](/wiki/Buddh%C4%81bhiseka "Buddhābhiseka") is a [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali") and [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") term referring to these consecration rituals.[[23]](#cite_note-buswell-23)

### Mormonism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mormonism")]

See also: [Law of consecration](/wiki/Law_of_consecration "Law of consecration") and [United Order](/wiki/United_Order "United Order")

[Mormonism](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism") is replete with consecration doctrine, primarily Christ's title of "The Anointed One" signifying his official, authorized and unique role as the savior of mankind from sin and death, and secondarily each individual's opportunity and ultimate responsibility to accept Jesus' will for their life and consecrate themselves to living thereby wholeheartedly. Book of Mormon examples include "sanctification cometh because of their yielding their hearts unto God" (Heleman 3:35) and "come unto Christ, who is the Holy One of Israel, and partake of his salvation, and the power of his redemption, ... and offer your whole souls as an offering unto him, and continue in fasting and praying, and endure to the end; and as the Lord liveth ye will be saved" (Omni 1:26).

### Hinduism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Hinduism")]

In most [South Indian](/wiki/South_India "South India") [Hindu temples](/wiki/Hindu_temple "Hindu temple") around the world, [Kumbhabhishekam](/wiki/Kumbhabhishekam "Kumbhabhishekam"), or the temple's consecration ceremony, is done once every 12 years. It is usually done to purify the temple after a [renovation](/wiki/Renovation "Renovation") or simply done to renew the purity of the temple. Hindus celebrate this event on the consecration date as the witnessing gives a good soul a thousand "punya", or [good karma](/wiki/Good_karma "Good karma").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Jainism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Jainism")]

*[Panch Kalyanaka Pratishtha Mahotsava](/wiki/Panch_Kalyanaka_Pratishtha_Mahotsava "Panch Kalyanaka Pratishtha Mahotsava")* is a traditional [Jain](/wiki/Jain "Jain") ceremony that consecrates one or more Jain [Tirthankara](/wiki/Tirthankara "Tirthankara") [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon") with celebration of [Panch Kalyanaka](/wiki/Panch_Kalyanaka "Panch Kalyanaka") (five auspicious events). The ceremony is generally held when a new Jain temple is erected or new idols are installed in temples.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The consecration must be supervised by a religious authority, an [Acharya](/wiki/Acharya "Acharya") or a [Bhattaraka](/wiki/Bhattaraka "Bhattaraka") or a scholar authorized by them.

## In academia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: In academia")]

### Hierology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hierology")]

See also: [Hierotopy](/wiki/Hierotopy "Hierotopy")

Hierology ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): ιερος, *hieros*, 'sacred or 'holy', + [-logy](/wiki/-logy "-logy")) is the study of [sacred literature](/wiki/Religious_text "Religious text") or [lore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) The concept and the term were developed in 2002 by [Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia") art-historian and [byzantinist](/wiki/Byzantinism "Byzantinism") [Alexei Lidov](/wiki/Alexei_Lidov "Alexei Lidov").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### History of religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: History of religions")]

Main article: [History of religions](/wiki/History_of_religions "History of religions")

Analysing the [dialectic](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic") of the sacred, [Mircea Eliade](/wiki/Mircea_Eliade "Mircea Eliade") outlines that religion should not be interpreted only as "belief in deities", but as "experience of the sacred."[[29]](#cite_note-29) The sacred is presented in relation to the profane;[[30]](#cite_note-30) the relation between the sacred and the profane is not of opposition, but of complementarity, as the profane is viewed as a [hierophany](/wiki/Hierophany "Hierophany").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Sociology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sociology")]

Main article: [Sacred–profane dichotomy](/wiki/Sacred%E2%80%93profane_dichotomy "Sacred–profane dichotomy")

French [sociologist](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") [Émile Durkheim](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Durkheim "Émile Durkheim") argued that the sacred appears to be connected to a higher realm but is in fact a symbolic encounter with society itself, produced and sustained through collective representations and ritual practices. Later scholars such as [Alan Mittleman](/wiki/Alan_Mittleman "Alan Mittleman") and [Mircea Eliade](/wiki/Mircea_Eliade "Mircea Eliade") modified this view by treating sacredness as an emergent phenomenon rather than a mere social illusion.[[32]](#cite_note-Mittleman-32) Durkheim also considered the [dichotomy](/wiki/Dichotomy "Dichotomy") between the sacred and the [profane](/wiki/Profanum "Profanum") to be the central characteristic of [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"): "religion is a unified system of beliefs and practices relative to *sacred things*, that is to say, things set apart and forbidden."[[33]](#cite_note-:0-33): 47 In Durkheim's theory, the sacred represented the interests of the group, especially unity, which were embodied in sacred group symbols, or [totems](/wiki/Totems "Totems"). The profane, on the other hand, involved mundane individual concerns. Durkheim explicitly stated that the dichotomy sacred/profane was not equivalent to [good/evil](/wiki/Good_and_evil "Good and evil"). The sacred could be [good](/wiki/Good "Good") or [evil](/wiki/Evil "Evil"), and the profane could be either as well.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## In religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: In religion")]

### Ancient religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ancient religions")]

Main article: [Sacrosanctity](/wiki/Sacrosanctity "Sacrosanctity")

In [ancient Roman religion](/wiki/Ancient_Roman_religion "Ancient Roman religion"), the concept of [sacrosanctity](/wiki/Sacrosanctity "Sacrosanctity") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *sacrosanctitas*) was extremely important in attempting to protect the [tribunes of the plebs](/wiki/Tribunes_of_the_plebs "Tribunes of the plebs") from personal harm. The tribunician power was later arrogated to the [emperors](/wiki/Roman_emperor "Roman emperor") in large part to provide them with the role's sacred protections. In addition to sanctifying temples and similar sanctuaries, the Romans also undertook the ritual of the *sulcus primigenius* when founding a new city—particularly [formal colonies](/wiki/Roman_colony "Roman colony")—in order to make the entire circuit of the town's wall ritually sacred as a further means of protection. In order to allow the removal of corpses to graveyards and similarly profane work, the city gates were left exempted from the rite.

### Indic religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Indic religions")]

See also: [Sacred groves of India](/wiki/Sacred_groves_of_India "Sacred groves of India"), [Sacred banyan trees of India](/wiki/List_of_Banyan_trees_in_India "List of Banyan trees in India"), [Sacred Vedic rivers](/wiki/Rigvedic_rivers "Rigvedic rivers"), and [Sacred Sapta Sindhu rivers](/wiki/Sapta_Sindhu "Sapta Sindhu")

[Indian-origin religion](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions"), namely [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and its offshoots [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") and [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), have concept of revering and conserving ecology and environment by treating various objects as sacred, such as rivers, trees, forests or groves, mountains, etc.

#### Hinduism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Hinduism")]

**Sacred rivers** and their reverence is a phenomenon found in several religions, especially religions which have [eco-friendly belief](/wiki/Animism "Animism") as core of their religion. For example, the [Indian-origin religions](/wiki/Indian_religions "Indian religions") ([Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), and [Sikism](/wiki/Sikism "Sikism")) revere and preserve the [groves](/wiki/Sacred_groves_of_India "Sacred groves of India"), [trees](/wiki/List_of_Banyan_trees_in_India "List of Banyan trees in India"), [mountains](/wiki/Sacred_mountains#India "Sacred mountains") and rivers as sacred. Among the most sacred rivers in Hinduism are the [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges "Ganges"),[[35]](#cite_note-Alter2001-35) [Yamuna](/wiki/Yamuna "Yamuna"),[[36]](#cite_note-hy-36)[[37]](#cite_note-brit-37) [Sarasvati](/wiki/Sarasvati_River "Sarasvati River")[[38]](#cite_note-EB-Sarasvati-38) rivers on which the [rigvedic rivers](/wiki/Rigvedic_rivers "Rigvedic rivers") flourished. The [vedas](/wiki/Vedas "Vedas") and [Gita](/wiki/Gita "Gita"), the most sacred of [hindu texts](/wiki/Hindu_texts "Hindu texts") were written on the banks of Sarasvati river which were codified during the [Kuru kingdom](/wiki/Kuru_Kingdom "Kuru Kingdom") in present-day [Haryana](/wiki/Haryana "Haryana"). Among other secondary sacred rivers of Hinduism are [Narmada](/wiki/Narmada "Narmada")[[39]](#cite_note-39) and many more.

Among the sacred mountains, the most sacred among those are [Mount Kailash](/wiki/Mount_Kailash "Mount Kailash")[[40]](#cite_note-40) (in Tibet), [Nanda Devi](/wiki/Nanda_Devi "Nanda Devi"), [Char Dham](/wiki/Char_Dham "Char Dham") mountains and [Amarnath](/wiki/Amarnath_temple "Amarnath temple") mountain, [Gangotri](/wiki/Gangotri "Gangotri") mountain. [Yamunotri](/wiki/Yamunotri "Yamunotri") mountain, [Sarasvotri](/wiki/Bandarpunch "Bandarpunch") mountain (origin of [Sarasvati River](/wiki/Sarasvati_River "Sarasvati River")), [Dhosi Hill](/wiki/Dhosi_Hill "Dhosi Hill"), etc.

#### Buddhism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Buddhism")]

In [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") Buddhism one finds the designation of *[ariya-puggala](/wiki/Ariya-Puggala "Ariya-Puggala")* ('noble person'). Buddha described the [Four stages of awakening](/wiki/Four_stages_of_awakening "Four stages of awakening") of a person depending on their level of purity. This purity is measured by which of the ten *[samyojana](/wiki/Fetter_(Buddhism) "Fetter (Buddhism)")* ('fetters') and *[klesha](/wiki/Kle%C5%9B%C4%81_(Buddhism) "Kleśā (Buddhism)")* have been purified and integrated from the [mindstream](/wiki/Mindstream "Mindstream"). These persons are called (in order of increasing sanctity) *[Sotāpanna](/wiki/Sot%C4%81panna "Sotāpanna")*, *[Sakadagami](/wiki/Sakadagami "Sakadagami")*, *[Anāgāmi](/wiki/An%C4%81g%C4%81mi "Anāgāmi")*, and *[Arahant](/wiki/Arhat "Arhat")*.

The range of [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denominations "Christian denominations") provide a wide variety of interpretations on sacredness. The [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), and [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") Churches, believe in [Holy Sacraments](/wiki/Holy_Sacraments "Holy Sacraments") that the clergy perform, such as [Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion") and [Holy Baptism](/wiki/Holy_Baptism "Holy Baptism"), as well as strong belief in the [Holy Catholic Church](/wiki/Four_marks_of_the_church "Four marks of the church"), [Holy Scripture](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), [Holy Trinity](/wiki/Holy_Trinity "Holy Trinity"), and the [Holy Covenant](/wiki/Covenant_(biblical) "Covenant (biblical)"). They also believe that angels and saints are [called to holiness](/wiki/Universal_call_to_holiness "Universal call to holiness"). In Methodist [Wesleyan theology](/wiki/Wesleyan_theology "Wesleyan theology") holiness has acquired the secondary meaning of the reshaping of a person through [entire sanctification](/wiki/Entire_sanctification "Entire sanctification"). The [Holiness movement](/wiki/Holiness_movement "Holiness movement") began within the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") Methodist church among those who thought the church had lost the zeal and emphasis on personal holiness of Wesley's day. Around the middle of the 20th century, the [Conservative Holiness Movement](/wiki/Conservative_Holiness_Movement "Conservative Holiness Movement"), a conservative offshoot of the Holiness movement, was born. The [Higher Life movement](/wiki/Higher_Life_movement "Higher Life movement") appeared in the British Isles during the mid-19th century.

Commonly recognized outward expressions or "standards" of holiness among more fundamental adherents frequently include applications relative to dress, hair, and appearance: e.g., short hair on men, uncut hair on women, and prohibitions against shorts, pants on women, make-up and jewelry. Other common injunctions are against places of worldly amusement, mixed swimming, smoking, [minced oaths](/wiki/Minced_oath "Minced oath"), as well as the eschewing of television and radio.

#### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Islam")]

See also: [Miqat](/wiki/Miqat "Miqat")

Among the [names of God in the Quran](/wiki/Names_of_God_in_Islam "Names of God in Islam") is *Al-Quddus* (القدوس): found in [Q59:23](/wiki/Q59:23 "Q59:23")[[41]](#cite_note-41) and [62:1](https://www.perseus.tufts.edu/hopper/text?doc=Perseus%3Atext%3A2002.02.0006%3Asura%3D62%3Averse%3D1), the closest English translation is 'holy' or 'sacred'. (It shares the same [triliteral Semitic root](/wiki/Triliteral_Semitic_root "Triliteral Semitic root"), [Q-D-Š](/wiki/Q-D-%C5%A0 "Q-D-Š"), as the Hebrew *[kodesh](/wiki/Holy_of_Holies "Holy of Holies")*.) Another use of the same root is found in the Arabic name for Jerusalem: *[al-Quds](/wiki/Al-Quds "Al-Quds")*, 'the Holy'.

The word *[ḥarām](/wiki/Haram "Haram")* (حرام), often translated as 'prohibited' or 'forbidden', is better understood as 'sacred' or 'sanctuary' in the context of places considered sacred in Islam. For example:

* the *[Masjid al-Haram](/wiki/Masjid_al-Haram "Masjid al-Haram")*, or the 'Sacred Mosque in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca")', constituting the immediate precincts of the [Kaaba](/wiki/Kaaba "Kaaba");
* *[al-Haramain](/wiki/Haram_(site) "Haram (site)")*, or 'the (two) Sanctuaries', a reference to the twin holy cities of Mecca and [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina"); and
* the *[Haram ash-Sharif](/wiki/Haram_ash-Sharif "Haram ash-Sharif")*, or 'Noble Sanctuary', the precincts of the [Dome of the Rock](/wiki/Dome_of_the_Rock "Dome of the Rock") and [al-Aqsa Mosque](/wiki/Qibli_Mosque "Qibli Mosque") in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem").

#### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sacredness&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Judaism")]

Main article: [Holiness in Judaism](/wiki/Holiness_in_Judaism "Holiness in Judaism")

See also: [Q-D-Š](/wiki/Q-D-%C5%A0 "Q-D-Š"), [Tzadik](/wiki/Tzadik "Tzadik"), and [Holy of Holies](/wiki/Holy_of_Holies "Holy of Holies")

The Hebrew word *kodesh* (קֹדֶשׁ) is used in the [Torah](/wiki/Torah "Torah") to mean "set apart" and "distinct." *Kodesh* is also commonly translated as "holy" and "sacred."[[42]](#cite_note-42) Holiness (*kedushah*) is presented as something that is actively produced through their relationship to God or through designation for sacred purposes. This relational understanding is reflected in both ritual law and everyday practices, such as marriage. Classical and modern thinkers alike emphasize that holiness is not a physical property but a status shaped by divine command, communal recognition, and human intention. More broadly, Jewish thought maintains that holiness is not confined to extraordinary places or moments but can permeate ordinary life through attentiveness, ethical action, and ritual practice. In this view, holiness emerges through cooperation between divine presence and human response, transforming the everyday world rather than rejecting it.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Westernwall2.jpg/250px-Westernwall2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westernwall2.jpg)

The [Western Wall](/wiki/Western_Wall "Western Wall"), one of the last relics of the [Temple in Jerusalem](/wiki/Temple_in_Jerusalem "Temple in Jerusalem")

Holiness is not a single state, but contains a broad spectrum. The [Mishnah](/wiki/Mishnah "Mishnah") lists concentric circles of holiness surrounding the [Temple in Jerusalem](/wiki/Temple_in_Jerusalem "Temple in Jerusalem"): [Holy of Holies](/wiki/Holy_of_Holies "Holy of Holies"), Temple Sanctuary, Temple Vestibule, Court of Priests, Court of Israelites, Court of Women, [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount"), the walled city of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), all the walled cities of Israel, and the borders of the [Land of Israel](/wiki/Land_of_Israel "Land of Israel").[[43]](#cite_note-43) Distinctions are made as to who and what are permitted in each area.

Time plays a particularly central role in Jewish conceptions of holiness, with [Shabbat](/wiki/Shabbat "Shabbat") and [Jewish holidays](/wiki/Jewish_holidays "Jewish holidays") regarded as among the most enduring and accessible form of sanctity after the Temple’s destruction. Biblical and rabbinic sources describe the Sabbath as holy independent of human observance, yet insist that preparation and behavioral distinctions are necessary to experience its sanctity fully. Work is not allowed on those days, and rabbinic tradition lists [39 categories of activity](/wiki/39_categories_of_activity_prohibited_on_Shabbat "39 categories of activity prohibited on Shabbat") that are specifically prohibited.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
